title: outlineR: Artefact Processing and Extraction Protocol
author: David Nicolas Matzig
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bygaptse

steps.title: For this project, create a new folder on your computer
steps.instruction: For this project, create a new folder on your computer. In the following protocol, this project folder will be referred to as "/project_folder".

steps.title: Inside of "/project_folder" create the following...
steps.instruction: Inside of "/project_folder" create the following subfolders: one for the raw images ("/project_folder/raw"), one for the prepared images ("/project_folder/prep"), one for the single artefacts as a subfolder of the prepared images folder ("/project_folder/prep/single"). Expected folder structure: | ├── project_folder/ │ ├── raw/ # raw image data │ └── prep/ # prepared image data │ └── single/ # single artefacts | ├── data/ │ ├── raw_data/ # data obtained from elsewhere │ └── derived_data/ # data generated during the analysis

steps.title: For the purpose of this protocol,...
steps.instruction: For the purpose of this protocol, we are going to use a picture of Morar Quartz Industry from Wellcome Collection ( https://wellcomecollection.org/works/th7egtfj ; CC BY 4.0). The image serves as proxy for a typical figure in a catalogue of an archaeological publication. Save it under "/project_folder/raw" as "morar_quartz_industry.jpg". When saving your raw image(s) make sure to save them in ".jpg" format with no special characters or empty spaces in their filename. Morar Quartz Industry (CC BY 4.0) Morar Quartz Industry ( https://wellcomecollection.org/works/th7egtfj ; CC BY 4.0)

steps.title: Typically, figures of archaeological (stone) artefacts...
steps.instruction: Typically, figures of archaeological (stone) artefacts contain scale bars, numberings, and other descriptions or details, or in the case of photographs, heterogenous backgrounds. This section describes, how to manually remove all elements in a picture, that do not belong to the artefact itself. For every raw image:

steps.title: Open GIMP GNU Image Manipulation Program (GIMP) 2
steps.instruction: Open GIMP GNU Image Manipulation Program (GIMP) 2.8 Ubuntu 18.04 GIMP Development Team

steps.title: Load raw, unprepared picture ("/project_folder/raw/morar_quartz_industry
steps.instruction: Load raw, unprepared picture ("/project_folder/raw/morar_quartz_industry.jpg").

steps.title: Remove all numbering, scales, text, ventral...
steps.instruction: Remove all numbering, scales, text, ventral and lateral views, incomplete artefacts, etc. using i.e. the Rectangle select tool in GIMP in combination with the "delete" key on your computer's keyboard.

steps.title: Check if picture is in grayscale. Go to
steps.instruction: Check if picture is in grayscale. Go to: Image → Mode → Grayscale

steps.title: Threshold/binarize the image to get clear,...
steps.instruction: Threshold/binarize the image to get clear, thick lines around the artefacts. Go to: Color → Threshold Check, whether there is some pixels in the picture outside of the artefacts that got created in the process of thresholding. If so, remove them. Check whether all lines around the artefacts are closed. If not, use the Pencil tool with Hardness 100 and Pixel Size 1 to fill the gaps.

steps.title: Expected result
steps.instruction: Expected result: Prepared image of Morar Quartz Industry ( https://wellcomecollection.org/works/th7egtfj ; CC BY 4.0)

steps.title: Save the prepared image under "/project_folder/prep"...
steps.instruction: Save the prepared image under "/project_folder/prep" using the same filename as the raw image. Save at 100% detail Do not change the size/scaling/dpi/... of the picture in any of the steps!

steps.title: Build a
steps.instruction: Build a .tps file into which all filenames of the prepared images are written. This file is needed for Step 4: Scaling Factor - Get Scaling Factor . This step requires tpsUtil32 by F. James Rohlf ( http://www.sbmorphometrics.org/soft-utility.html ). tpsUtil32 1.81 Microsoft Windows F. James Rohlf

steps.title: Start tpsUtil and navigate to
steps.instruction: Start tpsUtil and navigate to: Operations → Build tps file from images Such a .tps file must only be build, if all pictures in the respective folder are definite and no images are added or deleted. Ideally, the .tps file is build after all images have been prepared.

steps.title: In the field Input directory click on Input
steps.instruction: In the field Input directory click on Input : 1. navigate to “/project_folder/raw”, 2. select the first (or any) picture, 3. under Files of type select JPEG Bitmap, 4. click Open .

steps.title: In the field Output file click on Output
steps.instruction: In the field Output file click on Output : 1. navigate to “/project_folder/raw”, 2. create a .tps file with a sensible name that uses underscores instead of empty spaces, and no special characters.

steps.title: In the field Actions click on Setup
steps.instruction: In the field Actions click on Setup : 1. make sure to Include all , 2. do not include path , 3. Create .

steps.title: With tpsDig we are going to...
steps.instruction: With tpsDig we are going to derive the pixel-to-cm scaling factor, using the scale bars on the raw images. tpsDIG2w32 2.31 Microsoft Windows F. James Rohlf

steps.title: Load the .tps file
steps.instruction: Load the .tps file: File → Input source → File and select the .tps file created in tpsUtil in Step 3: Scaling Factor - Create TPS File .

steps.title: For each image, set the pixel to centimeter ratio
steps.instruction: For each image, set the pixel to centimeter ratio. In the “Image Tools” window ( Options → Image tools… ): 1. Set scale , 2. click on the starting point and the end point of the scale in the image, 3. type in the length of the measured scale/reference length, 4. make sure it’s the correct measure (i.e. centimeters, millimeters,..), 5. click OK . 6. If there is more than one raw image to scale, go to the next image by clicking on the red arrow pointing to the right in the top left corner.

steps.title: File → save data
steps.instruction: File → save data

steps.title: The R package outlineR (Matzig 2021)...
steps.instruction: The R package outlineR (Matzig 2021) is a helpful wrapper around functions from mainly the Momocs (v. 1.3.0; Bonhomme et al. 2014), EBImage (v. 4.28.1; Pau et al. 2010), and imager (v. 0.42.3; Barthelme et al. 2020) packages. It is designed for the fast and easy extraction of single outline shapes from images containing multiple thereof, such as the one we prepared in the steps above. To use the package we require a current version of the statistical programming language R (≥ 3.6.3; R Core Team 2020). An integrated development environment such as RStudio is recommended. R ≥ 3.6.3 R Core Team (2020) RStudio ≥ 1.2.5033 RStudio Team (2020) Inside of R, outlineR can be installed via the following command: # from https://github.com/yesdavid/outlineR remotes::install_github("yesdavid/outlineR", dependencies = TRUE) remotes::install_github("yesdavid/outlineR")

steps.title: Start Rstudio and create a new project
steps.instruction: Start Rstudio and create a new project: 1. File → New Project... → Existing Directory , 2. navigate to "/project_folder", 3. Create Project .

steps.title: Inside Rstudio, open an R script
steps.instruction: Inside Rstudio, open an R script: File → New File → R Script . Inside the R script we will execute all following commands.

steps.title: Load outlineR
steps.instruction: Load outlineR: library(outlineR)

steps.title: Define paths
steps.instruction: Define paths: # Define where the prepared images containing multiple artefacts are right now. inpath <- file.path(".", "prep") # Define where the single artefacts should be saved. outpath <- file.path(".", "prep", "single") # Define where the images containing multiple artefacts are right now. inpath <- "./test_data/input_data" # Define where the separate images should be saved. outpath <- "./test_data/derived_data" The paths as depicted here are relative paths. If there is no RStudio project set-up in the "project_folder"-folder as described above, the absolute (full) paths to the "/prep/" and "/single/" directories has to be provided instead.

steps.title: Extract the artefacts from the image(s)
steps.instruction: Extract the artefacts from the image(s): outlineR::separate_single_artefacts(inpath = inpath, outpath = outpath) separate_single_artefacts(inpath = inpath, outpath = outpath) Afterwards, the images of the single artefacts should be saved in the "./prep/single" folder which was defined as outpath in the code. The single artefact images get individual names based on the original file name and an extension consisting of "_pseudo_no_" and a consecutive number. The extraction process does not follow a left-to-right, top-to-bottom scheme and therefore the pseudo numbers will not reflect any (potential) prior numbering on the raw image. If there is empty images in the "./prep/single" folder, re-check the prepared images in "./prep" for single pixels outside the artefacts or open outlines. If necessary, delete all images in outpath. Then, re-run this command.

steps.title: Import the .tps-file
steps.instruction: Import the .tps-file: tps_df <- outlineR::tps_to_df(file.path("path", "to", "file.tps") tps_df <- tps_to_df("path/to/tps/file.tps") If no .tps-file exists, skip this step.

steps.title: Extract the outlines of all separated...
steps.instruction: Extract the outlines of all separated artefacts and combine them with their scaling factor: single_outlines_list <- outlineR::get_outlines(outpath = outpath, tps_file_rescale = tps_df) single_outlines_list <- get_outlines(outpath = outpath, tps_file_rescale = tps_df) If no .tps-file exists, run the following command: single_outlines_list <- outlineR::get_outlines(outpath = outpath, tps_file_rescale = NULL) single_outlines_list <- get_outlines(outpath = outpath, tps_file_rescale = tps_df)

steps.title: Combine the single outlines into a...
steps.instruction: Combine the single outlines into a common "Out" file (a specific filetype from the Momocs R package): outlines_combined <- outlineR::combine_outlines(single_outlines_list = single_outlines_list) outlines_combined <- combine_outlines(single_outlines_list = single_outlines_list)

steps.title: In a last step before starting...
steps.instruction: In a last step before starting to work with your outlines, you should inspect the just created outlines for validity and possible errors: length(outlines_combined) #how many outlines do you have? stack(outlines_combined) # shows all outlines above one another(you might want to center and scale them first using Momocs) Momocs::panel(outlines_combined) # shows all outlines next to each other Momocs::inspect(outlines_combined) # shows only a single outline at a time. Expected result: Stacked outlines. All extracted outlines from outlines_combined visualised using the Momocs::panel() command.
